soil_reasoning: Northern plains are predominantly clay soils from alluvial deposits, ideal for wet paddy cultivation in the MADA irrigation zone.
Inland valley area with clay loam from weathered granite, suitable for fruit trees and mixed crops.
Similar to Kedah plains, heavy clay soils suitable for paddy with MADA-extension irrigation.
Coastal alluvial plain with mixed clay loam soils, supports diversified agriculture.
Flat alluvial plain in KADA irrigation area, heavy clay ideal for wet paddy.
Valley area surrounded by limestone hills, sandy loam from alluvial deposits, good drainage for vegetables.
Central peninsular Malaysia has clay loam soils from weathered granite, good for wide range of crops.
Coastal peat soils requiring careful water management. High organic matter but acidic.
Hilly terrain with clay loam from granite parent material, suitable for highland vegetables.
Low-lying coastal area with heavy clay soils, traditionally used for rice and oil palm.
Inland area with laterite-derived sandy loam, well-drained, suitable for fruits and vegetables.
East coast with sandy loam from coastal sediment deposits, suitable for mixed crops and some paddy.
Coastal sandy soils requiring organic matter amendment. Good for cash crops with proper irrigation.
Coastal area with sandy loam soils, influenced by northeast monsoon with high seasonal rainfall.
Highland area (1500m elevation) with rich loamy soils from granite weathering, ideal for temperate vegetables.
Inland river valley with fertile clay loam from alluvial deposits along Pahang River.
Undulating terrain with clay loam soils, good for mixed agriculture and smallholder farming.

West coast Sabah with clay loam from sedimentary rock weathering, supports diverse crops.
